main_summary.summary: Mary Frances Jenkins, born August 12, 1928 in Waycross, Georgia, recounts her childhood in Albany under Jim Crow, her education at Madison High School and Fisk University, her wartime service that took her to Fort Dix and Fort Custer, and her teaching career at East Baker High School and Carver Junior High School. After returning to Albany following her divorce from Roscoe Jenkins, she witnessed two formative incidents in Baker County: a white supervisor firing a young Black male teacher on the spot, and the same supervisor lecturing Black teachers about desegregation by declaring she would never invite one of them to her home. Jenkins joined the Albany Movement after attending the first mass meeting at Mount Zion Baptist Church under Rev. James Grant, then volunteered in the office set up at Dr. William G. Anderson's house, where Martin Luther King Jr. handed her Harry Belafonte's phone number to call. She describes taking notes on church fans when she ran out of paper at mass meetings, material that later became her self-published book Open Themselves: A Pictorial History of the Albany Civil Rights Movement. Interview conducted March 9, 2013 by Willie Griffin (Southern Oral History Program) on the campus of Albany State University.
key_themes: mass meetings at Mount Zion Baptist Church
documenting the movement from the inside
Madison High School and segregated education
main_summary.historical_significance: Jenkins provides first-person testimony from the office-side of the Albany Movement that SNCC field workers and pulpit leaders rarely describe. Her account of placing the call to Harry Belafonte at Dr. King's direction, her observation of King's meetings with women in which he posed a question and then listened, and her practice of taking notes on cardboard church fans when she ran out of paper at mass meetings are canonical details of Movement office mechanics. Her two Baker County incidents (a Black male teacher fired on the spot by the white supervisor; the same supervisor's post-Brown speech to Black teachers) are first-person evidence of the pre-Civil-Rights-Act vulnerability of Black school employees in Southwest Georgia. Her self-published book Open Themselves, drawn from those fan-notes, is a primary-source artifact of the Albany Movement now anchored in the LoC oral history collection.

chapters.part: Waycross to Albany, a Jim Crow Childhood
chapters.title: Born in Waycross, Raised in Albany
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Willie Griffin at Albany State University in March 2013, Mary Frances Jenkins gives her birth in Waycross, Georgia on August 12, 1928, a young childhood in Pittsburgh after her father was killed in an automobile accident on a Georgia highway, and the move to Albany around age eight or nine, where she entered the fourth grade, after her mother remarried.

chapters.part: Waycross to Albany, a Jim Crow Childhood
chapters.title: You Can't Has Followed My Life
chapters.summary: Jenkins recounts growing up in the Jim Crow era, sitting in the balcony of the Albany movie theater and drinking from separate fountains, a white boy squirting a water pistol in her face while her mother held her hand, having to tell her own three-year-old daughter the same word her mother told her, you can't, when the child wanted a whites-only ride, and the homemaker mother who never finished high school but insisted education was the key, married to a stepfather who worked on the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad.

chapters.title: The Best Teachers in the World
chapters.summary: Jenkins remembers Madison High School as an all-Black segregated school with hand-me-down books and outdated equipment but, she insists, the best teachers in the world, naming a strict principal Ruth T. Kimball and English teacher Olive Rogers, the firm non-abusive discipline of that era, serving as editor-in-chief of the high school annual, graduating as salutatorian with her friend Vashti Stubbs steadying her nerves, and her early ambition to be a writer of fiction novels before settling on teaching.

chapters.title: A Petition on the First Day
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes choosing Fisk University in Nashville on a teacher's recommendation and her family's financial sacrifice, being handed a petition to oust a teacher almost her first day on campus, a teacher she signed against but who became the best she ever had, and her involvement in creative writing and the little theater, directing a teacher's play her senior year.

chapters.title: From the Service Back to a Classroom
chapters.topic: Military Years and a First Job
chapters.summary: She tells of leaving Fisk to spend two years in the service, marrying Roscoe Jenkins, basic training in Virginia, special services at Fort Dix, and a still-segregated posting at Fort Custer in Michigan, then after the birth of her daughter Sherry returning to finish at Fisk, divorcing Roscoe of North Carolina and later remarrying, losing a promised Albany job over a certificate mismatch, and being hired by her pastor Rev. James Grant, principal at East Baker High School.

chapters.title: I Would Never Invite One of You to My Home
chapters.summary: At Baker County she says she learned what white power could do through two incidents: a white supervisor, wife of the county school superintendent, firing a young teacher on the spot with no recourse over a weekend incident, and the same supervisor, the year the Supreme Court ruled on school desegregation, assembling the Black teachers to argue against integration and declaring she would never invite one of them to her home.

chapters.title: Army Barracks for a Classroom
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes leaving Baker County for Carver Junior High School in Dougherty County, teaching two months in army barracks behind the building before getting a room, still using hand-me-down second books, and her view, looking at the gangs of today, that discipline in the late 1950s was both possible and needed.

chapters.title: A Biracial Committee Refused
chapters.summary: She recalls the quiet pre-movement activity in Black Albany, a group of men in the Criterion Club asking the City Commission for a biracial committee and being turned down, Dr. William G. Anderson refused over flooding in his neighborhood, the movement's own representatives later told the commission had no way to form such a committee, the all-negative portrayal of Blacks in the white-owned Albany Herald, and the locally edited Southwest Georgian as the community's only Black paper.

chapters.title: The Crowds, the Singing, the Crying
chapters.topic: The First Mass Meeting and King's Office
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes going to the first mass meeting at Mount Zion Baptist Church under Rev. James Grant, the crowds, singing, and crying, song leaders Charles Sherrod, Bernice Johnson Reagon, and Rutha Mae Harris, her wish to get segregation out of her children's lives, and volunteering in the office set up in Dr. William G. Anderson's house for Martin Luther King Jr., answering the phone and once dialing a number King handed her for the calypso singer Harry Belafonte.

chapters.title: Notes Written on Fans
chapters.summary: Jenkins recalls King's meetings with women, whom he called the movers and shakers of the world, and how, listening to people return from jail describing their treatment, she realized the unnamed many would be forgotten and began taking notes, sometimes on paper fans when her purse ran out, material she hopes one day to give to the museum, the seed of her book.

chapters.title: My Husband Pulled Me Out of the Line
chapters.summary: She describes the threat that arrested teachers would be fired and rumors of people checking car tags at mass meetings, her decision not to march, and the one Sunday she nearly did, rising with her four-year-old son in her arms after Rev. James Grant left the pulpit to act alone, until her husband gently pulled her from the line, the same day Chief Laurie Pritchett chose to arrest no one.

chapters.title: Carry the Report Cards to the Principal
chapters.summary: Jenkins relates the humiliations Black teachers faced once integration began, some isolated with no colleagues, others required to carry report cards to the white principal so he could check whether white students had been given proper grades, then turns to the book she wrote, prompted by her late daughter's urging that she assemble her material.

chapters.title: I Had to Pay for Every Mistake
chapters.summary: She describes how, with her material already organized, assembling the book was easy, but she knew nothing of publishing and took it to a religious-book company in Columbus, Georgia, where a copy editor she believes disliked the content introduced errors Jenkins had to pay to fix, until she sold out the first edition.

chapters.title: They Used Third Edition
chapters.topic: A Dropped Picture and a Poetry Book
chapters.summary: Jenkins recounts finding, before a book signing, a page printed with a blank spot where a directed picture was never dropped in, deciding to hold the signing and promise corrected copies, the publisher reprinting and labeling it the third edition, the warm community response that carried the book to a fourth edition, and her new project retelling the movement in poetry because children respond to it.

chapters.title: The Laws That Helped Us Are Challenged Now
chapters.summary: Asked about the Albany movement's impact, Jenkins says she cannot know how long change would have taken without it, points to Black people working behind store counters as proof of what was won, warns the journey is not over, and closes on Martin Luther King Jr.'s legacy of service, the students who entered the bus station to enforce the Interstate Commerce Commission ban on segregated terminals, the six girls who integrated the Albany high school after C.B. King's eight-year struggle, and her warning that the laws that once helped are now being challenged.

loc_video.caption: Mary Jenkins oral history interview conducted by Will Griffin in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
